pred_reason: The label set SWC-101 (Integer Overflow and Underflow) and SWC-107 (Reentrancy) is reasonable because the contract exhibits unprotected arithmetic operations vulnerable to overflow/underflow and shows patterns susceptible to reentrancy attacks. Evidence: Unprotected multiplication in decreaseAllowance() without SafeMath (SWC-101); Transfer function follows dangerous state-change-before-event pattern (SWC-107); Inconsistent SafeMath usage leaves some operations vulnerable (SWC-101)

findings.Description: The _transfer function follows a dangerous pattern of state changes followed by event emission, making it susceptible to reentrancy attacks if extended or modified. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential for incorrect token accounting and balance inconsistencies if reentrancy occurs.

findings.Description: The decreaseAllowance() function performs direct multiplication without SafeMath protection. This issue appears in the smart contract code and was identified in static analysis.
findings.Impact: Potential overflow with large inputs could corrupt the rTotal value.

findings.Description: setrouteChain() and Approve() functions lack zero address validation when setting critical addresses. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Could accidentally disable token transfers or break approval mechanisms if set to zero address.

findings.Description: Owner has unrestricted power to mint tokens, change transfer limits, and bypass restrictions via caller/router addresses. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Owner could manipulate token economics and bypass intended restrictions.

findings.Description: Contract maintains two owner variables (_owner and Owner) that can become inconsistent. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Confusion about actual owner status and potential issues with owner-only functions.

findings.Description: decreaseAllowance() changes state variable rTotal without emitting an event. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Reduced transparency and difficulty tracking changes off-chain.

findings.Description: Approved addresses can bypass transfer limits when sending to router address. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Privileged addresses could drain liquidity or manipulate token flow.

findings.Description: Contract lacks protection against max uint256 values and zero transfers. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential for dusting attacks, unlimited approvals, and unintended minting.

findings.Description: Some arithmetic operations use SafeMath while others don't. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential overflow/underflow vulnerabilities in unprotected operations.

findings.Description: Transfer functions don't protect against malicious contract recipients. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential for reentrancy attacks if external calls are added.
